message.content:
Template Injection is a technique where adversaries manipulate document templates to conceal and execute malicious code or force authentication attempts. This method leverages the Office Open XML (OOXML) format used in Microsoft Office documents (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx) and can also exploit Rich Text Format (RTF) files. By injecting malicious URLs into template properties, adversaries can fetch and execute payloads when the document is opened, often evading static detection mechanisms. Common delivery methods include phishing and tainted shared content. Notable groups like APT28, Chaes, Confucius, and Lazarus Group have exploited this technique to deploy various payloads, including macros and remote templates.

The potential impact includes unauthorized code execution, data exfiltration, and forced authentication, which can lead to credential theft. Mitigation strategies involve disabling automatic template loading, employing robust email filtering, and using endpoint protection solutions to detect and block suspicious document activities. Regularly updating software and educating users about phishing risks are also crucial in reducing the effectiveness of template injection attacks.
